reasoning: The current 30-day SOFR average as of late May (around 4.315) is elevated relative to current daily SOFR rates (4.26). This is because higher daily rates from early May and late April are still heavily weighting the 30-day rolling average. By June 1, these older rates will drop out of the 30-day window and be replaced by rates in the 4.26 range, making it mathematically nearly certain the moving average will decrease, barring an unprecedented mid-month rate spike. For the June 24 window, even if SOFR experiences a liquidity-driven jump around mid-June tax dates, it is highly unlikely to average enough to cross the May 25 baseline. Looking toward the August 23 and November 21 target dates, market expectations strongly point to two or three Fed rate cuts throughout summer and fall. If the Fed cuts the target rate, SOFR will fall well below current levels. A resolution of YES requires the Fed to refrain from cutting rates AND for SOFR to independently drift significantly upwards from 4.26, or for the Fed to outright hike rates. The probability of an increase is therefore very low across all dates, though horizon uncertainty (i.e. inflation flaring up, delaying cuts or prompting a hike later in the year) pushes probabilities for August and November marginally closer to 0.5.
